questions.question: When does value-based RL (argmax over Q) break down?
options: Whenever rewards are negative

With continuous actions (which argmax over a 10-dimensional torque?) and when you want stochastic policies (argmax is deterministic by construction) — policy gradients instead parameterize π_θ(a|s) directly and ascend on expected return
When episodes exceed 100 steps
When the state is an image
questions.explanation: No argmax, no Bellman recursion — just gradient ascent on J(θ) = E[G]. Every LLM-RL algorithm in 2026 (PPO, DPO, GRPO) is a refinement of this idea.

questions.question: What does the REINFORCE theorem say?
options: Returns must be normalized to [0,1]
∇J(θ) = E[Σ_t G_t · ∇_θ log π_θ(a_t|s_t)] — run episodes, weight each action's log-probability gradient by the return that followed, average, step uphill
Q-values converge under Robbins-Monro
Policies must be deterministic at optimum
questions.explanation: The proof is two lines: the log-derivative trick ∇P = P·∇log P plus the fact that environment transition terms don't depend on θ and vanish.

questions.question: Why is baseline subtraction unbiased, and what's the typical baseline?
options: It isn't — it introduces small bias

E[b(s_t)·∇log π(a_t|s_t)] = 0 for any baseline that doesn't depend on the action, so subtracting b(s_t) changes variance but not the expected gradient — the typical choice is a learned V̂(s_t), which turns REINFORCE into actor-critic
Baselines only work for discrete actions
The baseline replaces the return entirely
questions.explanation: Vanilla REINFORCE's variance is murderous (noisy returns × noisy gradients). Baseline + reward-to-go gives the form that's the direct ancestor of A2C and PPO.

questions.question: What is the reward-to-go trick?
options: Discounting future rewards more heavily
Weight each action's gradient only by returns from time t ONWARD — past rewards can't be caused by the current action, so including them just adds zero-mean noise
Paying out rewards at episode end
Clipping returns to a fixed range
questions.explanation: A pure variance reduction grounded in causality: credit assignment should only flow backward from future outcomes, never from rewards already banked.

questions.question: What does ∇log π look like for a softmax policy?
options: The raw score of the taken action
∇f_θ(s, a_taken) minus the policy-weighted average Σ_{a'} π(a'|s)∇f_θ(s,a') — the score of the action taken minus its expected value under the policy
The Hessian of the value function
A one-hot vector of the action index
questions.explanation: "Push up what you did, pull down what you'd typically do" — and for continuous control, the Gaussian policy N(μ_θ(s), σ_θ(s)) gives the closed-form analogue SAC builds on.
